1	# Commission Log — Standing Research Instrument
2	#
3	# One entry per patron-sourced commission. Patron-subjective judgments only.
4	# Full artifacts live in experiments/data/commissions/<id>/ — see README there.
5	#
6	# IDs are writ IDs (w-*) from the Clerk. Pre-Clerk commissions used session
7	# IDs (ses-*) or ad-hoc labels — these are grandfathered with a note field.
8	#
9	# Fields:
10	#   id                — writ ID (canonical) or legacy session/label ID
11	#   title             — short description of the commissioned work
12	#   codex             — target codex name
13	#   complexity        — patron estimate (Fibonacci: 1 2 3 5 8 13 21)
14	#   spec_quality_pre  — patron assessment of spec before dispatch
15	#   outcome           — patron assessment after review
16	#   revision_required — whether follow-up work was needed
17	#   spec_quality_post — patron assessment of spec after review
18	#   reviewed_at       — ISO date when patron reviewed this commission (null = not reviewed)
19	#                       Backfill note: all commissions through w-mnjp1ph3 were reviewed by
20	#                       the patron (with Coco) on the dates shown. Dates are approximate,
21	#                       derived from review-related commit timestamps. After w-mnjp1ph3,
22	#                       patron review became ad hoc — the planning workshop and static
23	#                       implement→review→revise rig structure reduced perceived need.
24	#   failure_mode      — if outcome != success, what went wrong (omit if n/a)
25	#   note              — meta info about the record itself (not content)
26	#
27	# Scales:
28	#   Complexity (Fibonacci): 1 2 3 5 8 13 21
29	#   Spec quality: strong | adequate | weak
30	#   Outcome: success | partial | wrong | abandoned
31	#   Failure mode: spec_ambiguous | requirement_wrong | execution_error | complexity_overrun | broken | incomplete

# Commission Log — Standing Research Instrument
#
# One entry per patron-sourced commission. Patron-subjective judgments only.
# Full artifacts live in experiments/data/commissions/<id>/ — see README there.
#
# IDs are writ IDs (w-*) from the Clerk. Pre-Clerk commissions used session
# IDs (ses-*) or ad-hoc labels — these are grandfathered with a note field.
#
# Fields:
#   id                — writ ID (canonical) or legacy session/label ID
#   title             — short description of the commissioned work
#   codex             — target codex name
#   complexity        — patron estimate (Fibonacci: 1 2 3 5 8 13 21)
#   spec_quality_pre  — patron assessment of spec before dispatch
#   outcome           — patron assessment after review
#   revision_required — whether follow-up work was needed
#   spec_quality_post — patron assessment of spec after review
#   reviewed_at       — ISO date when patron reviewed this commission (null = not reviewed)
#                       Backfill note: all commissions through w-mnjp1ph3 were reviewed by
#                       the patron (with Coco) on the dates shown. Dates are approximate,
#                       derived from review-related commit timestamps. After w-mnjp1ph3,
#                       patron review became ad hoc — the planning workshop and static
#                       implement→review→revise rig structure reduced perceived need.
#   failure_mode      — if outcome != success, what went wrong (omit if n/a)
#   note              — meta info about the record itself (not content)
#
# Scales:
#   Complexity (Fibonacci): 1 2 3 5 8 13 21
#   Spec quality: strong | adequate | weak
#   Outcome: success | partial | wrong | abandoned
#   Failure mode: spec_ambiguous | requirement_wrong | execution_error | complexity_overrun | broken | incomplete

3	Session: 0410a5f6-2cfd-48de-8c32-ee20cddfa59d
4	
5	## What we did
6	
7	- **Designed the `patron-input` block type** — a new Spider block type that lets engines pose structured questions to the patron, block until answered, then resume. Iterated through question schema, answer discrimination, request lifecycle, and engine resume mechanics.
8	- **Designed the block checker failure signal** — widening `BlockType.check()` from `boolean` to `CheckResult` (`'cleared' | 'pending' | 'failed'`). Sean implemented this himself during the session (`f1864a1` in nexus).
9	- **Produced three artifacts:**
10	  - `.scratch/patron-input-block-type-brief.md` — mountain-shaped brief (authoritative intent)
11	  - `.scratch/patron-input-spec-candidate.md` — detailed prescriptive spec with full types/examples (reference)
12	  - `.scratch/block-checker-failure-signal-brief.md` — brief for the checker failure signal (already implemented)
13	
14	## Decisions made and/or deferred
15	
16	- **Choice answers use discriminated objects** (`{ selected: string } | { custom: string }`) not bare strings — prevents ambiguity between typo'd option keys and intentional custom answers.
17	- **`allowCustom` flag on choice questions** — mirrors planning workshop's need for write-in answers.
18	- **Two-phase completion** — answering questions doesn't unblock; patron explicitly calls `input-complete` or `input-reject`.
19	- **Rejected status triggers checker failure** — cascades engine → rig → writ failure through existing infrastructure.
20	- **Engine resume via book query** (`rigId + engineId + status:completed`, most recent) rather than `priorBlock` — survives process restarts, handles multi-block naturally.
21	- **`rigId` must be added to `EngineRunContext`** — folded into the patron-input commission since it didn't make it into the checker-failure one.
22	- **Brief goes through planning pipeline** rather than using the spec candidate directly — the spec candidate was built conversationally and has gaps the planner will catch.
23	- **CDC-driven unblocking deferred** — 10s polling is the mechanism; CDC is a future optimization for all block types.
24	
25	## Next steps & open questions
26	
27	- Dispatch the patron-input commission through the planning pipeline with the brief
28	- The checker-failure-signal brief (`.scratch/block-checker-failure-signal-brief.md`) is historical — commission already shipped
29	
30	### Notable moments
31	
32	- Sean's catch on choice answer ambiguity: *"if the answer is 'd' it would be interpreted as custom, but might have just been a mistaken attempt at selecting an option"* — led to the discriminated answer type.
33	- Sean pushed for proper string union on checker results: *"i don't like the `true | false | 'failed'` type. The true/false was already confusing (which is which!?) and this makes it worse"* — resulted in `CheckResult` with named statuses.
34	- Sean's question about multi-block and deterministic IDs surfaced a real gap in the resume mechanics that led to the query-based approach.

# Session Summary — 2026-04-07T142121

Session: 0410a5f6-2cfd-48de-8c32-ee20cddfa59d

## What we did

- **Designed the `patron-input` block type** — a new Spider block type that lets engines pose structured questions to the patron, block until answered, then resume. Iterated through question schema, answer discrimination, request lifecycle, and engine resume mechanics.
- **Designed the block checker failure signal** — widening `BlockType.check()` from `boolean` to `CheckResult` (`'cleared' | 'pending' | 'failed'`). Sean implemented this himself during the session (`f1864a1` in nexus).
- **Produced three artifacts:**
  - `.scratch/patron-input-block-type-brief.md` — mountain-shaped brief (authoritative intent)
  - `.scratch/patron-input-spec-candidate.md` — detailed prescriptive spec with full types/examples (reference)
  - `.scratch/block-checker-failure-signal-brief.md` — brief for the checker failure signal (already implemented)

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **Choice answers use discriminated objects** (`{ selected: string } | { custom: string }`) not bare strings — prevents ambiguity between typo'd option keys and intentional custom answers.
- **`allowCustom` flag on choice questions** — mirrors planning workshop's need for write-in answers.
- **Two-phase completion** — answering questions doesn't unblock; patron explicitly calls `input-complete` or `input-reject`.
- **Rejected status triggers checker failure** — cascades engine → rig → writ failure through existing infrastructure.
- **Engine resume via book query** (`rigId + engineId + status:completed`, most recent) rather than `priorBlock` — survives process restarts, handles multi-block naturally.
- **`rigId` must be added to `EngineRunContext`** — folded into the patron-input commission since it didn't make it into the checker-failure one.
- **Brief goes through planning pipeline** rather than using the spec candidate directly — the spec candidate was built conversationally and has gaps the planner will catch.
- **CDC-driven unblocking deferred** — 10s polling is the mechanism; CDC is a future optimization for all block types.

## Next steps & open questions

- Dispatch the patron-input commission through the planning pipeline with the brief
- The checker-failure-signal brief (`.scratch/block-checker-failure-signal-brief.md`) is historical — commission already shipped

### Notable moments

- Sean's catch on choice answer ambiguity: *"if the answer is 'd' it would be interpreted as custom, but might have just been a mistaken attempt at selecting an option"* — led to the discriminated answer type.
- Sean pushed for proper string union on checker results: *"i don't like the `true | false | 'failed'` type. The true/false was already confusing (which is which!?) and this makes it worse"* — resulted in `CheckResult` with named statuses.
- Sean's question about multi-block and deterministic IDs surfaced a real gap in the resume mechanics that led to the query-based approach.

### Experiment observations

- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** Pure design collaboration — no implementation by Coco. Sean implemented the checker failure signal himself during the session. The patron-input brief was a co-design exercise: Coco drafted, Sean challenged and refined. No commissionable work this session.

1	# Session Summary — 2026-04-07T161500
2	
3	Session: 4cddad8c-1f6f-4920-80dd-65917eb7ab37
4	
5	## What we did
6	
7	- **Diagnosed the plan-writer patron-override-drop problem.** Traced the D11 incident (commission w-mnnrgbdb) to root cause: the writer received the `patron_override` field but was overwhelmed by competing signals — the analyst's rationale, the brief text, and the option descriptions all argued for the opposite of the override.
8	- **Implemented three-layer fix** across two commits:
9	  1. **Decisions digest** (`generateDecisionsDigest()` in `bin/plan-review.ts`) — mechanically strips analyst reasoning, producing flat question→answer pairs. Patron overrides become `justification: "patron specified"` with no competing context. Generated before writer launch.
10	  2. **Decision compliance check** (new Step 4 in `bin/plan-prompts/planner.md`) — writer must audit its own spec point-by-point against the digest, quoting implementing text and flagging contradictions/omissions/dilution.
11	  3. **Brief removed from writer prompt** + **explicit authority hierarchy** added: patron decisions > analyst decisions > scope/inventory. Brief acknowledged as analyst input, not writer input.
12	- **Commits:** `53fc6a9` (digest + compliance check), `a70fa25` (brief removal + authority hierarchy).
13	- **Updated** `.scratch/todo/planner-patron-override-dropped.md` to RESOLVED with root cause and fix.
14	
15	## Decisions made and/or deferred
16	
17	- **Brief should not reach the writer** — it's the analyst's input, not the writer's. The scope, decisions, and inventory fully describe what to build.
18	- **Three-tier authority hierarchy** for the writer: patron decisions (highest) > analyst decisions > scope/brief (lowest, reference only).
19	- **Digest is mechanically generated** — no LLM in the transform. Simple YAML→YAML in plan-review.ts.
20	
21	## Next steps & open questions
22	
23	- Test the full pipeline end-to-end on the next commission that goes through the plan workshop.
24	- The D11 incident's spec (`configurable-rig-templates-via-guild/spec.md`) still contains the wrong `$role` references — the fix prevents future occurrences but doesn't retroactively fix that spec.
25	
26	### Notable moments
27	
28	- Sean's probe — "what instructions does the writer get regarding the brief?" — surfaced that the brief was being passed directly in the writer's input prompt, actively contradicting the patron override. The brief said `$role` was a "well-known reference"; the override said "there is no special $role variable." Three sources of contradictory signal hitting the writer simultaneously.
29	- The layered defense approach: digest (reduce noise), compliance check (catch what slips through), brief removal + authority hierarchy (eliminate the strongest contradictory signal entirely).
30	
31	### Experiment observations
32	
33	- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** Coco modified plan-writer infrastructure (sanctum tooling). The design was collaborative — Sean proposed the digest and self-check ideas, Coco analyzed tradeoffs and implemented. Sean's follow-up question about the brief led to the authority hierarchy, which was Sean's framing ("preference order for requirements"). All work is sanctum-side tooling, not commissionable.
34

1	# Session Summary — 2026-04-07T193000
2	
3	Session: 642e85ea-a2c2-40bf-9244-63e0b72d442d
4	
5	## What we did
6	
7	- **Designed The Oculus** — a new web dashboard apparatus for the Nexus framework. Three rounds of interactive design discussion covering plugin model, web framework, REST API shape, styling approach, and naming.
8	- **Created commission brief** at `.scratch/oculus-brief.md` — distilled design decisions into a planner-ready brief. Sean edited to trim sanctum-specific references from the Problem section.
9	- **Created placeholder architecture doc** at `/workspace/nexus/docs/architecture/apparatus/oculus.md` — follows the apparatus template, embeds all key design decisions as authoritative constraints. (Uncommitted — blocked by diverged nexus branch.)
10	- **Created and discarded full spec** at `.scratch/oculus-spec.md` — initially wrote a 28-requirement spec, then pivoted to a brief for the plan workshop instead.
11	- **Discovered merge conflict** in nexus repo — `main` has diverged with conflicts in three spider files from the block-checker-failure-signal commission. Aborted rebase, flagged for resolution.
12	
13	## Decisions made and/or deferred
14	
15	- **Hono** as web framework — zero deps, built-in TS, selected over Express (65 deps), Fastify (42 deps, competing plugin model), Koa (30 deps + separate router).
16	- **Pages only, not widgets** — plugins contribute full pages as static asset directories. No cross-plugin layout coordination.
17	- **CLI-mirror REST naming** (`/api/writ/list`) over RESTful (`/api/writs`) — mechanical transform, zero ambiguity, one mental model across CLI and web.
18	- **`'cli'` caller type renamed to `'patron'`** — eliminates need for a new `'web'` caller type. Both CLI and web are patron-facing interfaces.
19	- **HTTP method from permission level** — `read` → GET, `write`/`admin` → POST, `delete` → DELETE.
20	- **Server-side chrome injection** — Oculus injects nav and stylesheet into page `index.html` responses.
21	- **Shared CSS, three layers** — custom properties, element-type selectors, utility classes. Tokyo Night dark palette.
22	- **The Oculus** as the name (not Astrolabe) — Astrolabe reserved for a future planning-workflow apparatus.
23	- **Deferred:** w-mnonwi2y (Block Checker Failure Signal) outcome still null in commission log. Nexus branch conflict unresolved.
24	
25	## Next steps & open questions
26	
27	- **Resolve nexus branch conflict** before dispatching — the architecture doc needs to be committed, and the anima needs a clean branch.
28	- **Review planner output** when the workshop finishes processing the brief.
29	- **w-mnonwi2y outcome** needs recording.
30	
31	### Notable moments
32	
33	- Sean's push on `callableBy`: *"is there a change we could make to 'callableBy' to eliminate needing 'web' as its own thing? since 'web' is only read by an optional plugin, it seems weird to encode that into the tools-apparatus."* — Led to the `'cli'` → `'patron'` rename, which made the model cleaner and more honest. The optional plugin shouldn't leak into core vocabulary.
34	- Sean's redirect on the spec: *"that's.... pretty detailed. is this just a spec we dispatch directly then? not a brief we iterate on with our planner?"* — Coco had over-produced, writing a full spec when a brief was the right artifact. Led to trimming down and feeding the workshop.
35	- Sean catching the DELETE oversight: *"should 'delete' permission verbs use delete http verb?"* — Coco had lazily collapsed everything non-read into POST.
36	
37	### Experiment observations
38	
39	- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** Coco did design analysis (framework comparison with real npm data, tool system code review) and document authoring (brief, architecture doc, discarded spec). All sanctum-side. Sean directed the key decisions — Hono selection, pages-not-widgets, CLI-mirror naming, `'patron'` rename, DELETE verb. The brief was sent to the plan workshop for autonomous expansion.

content.content:
1 --- 2 author: plan-writer 3 estimated_complexity: 13 4 --- 5 6 # The Oculus — Web Dashboard Apparatus 7 8 ## Summary 9 10 A new apparatus (`@shardworks/oculus-apparatus`) that serves a web dashboard for the guild via Hono. Plugins contribute pages as static asset directories and custom API routes through kit contributions. Guild tools are automatically exposed as REST endpoints. A shared stylesheet and injected navigation chrome give contributed pages a cohesive appearance with zero boilerplate. Prerequisite: the `ToolCaller` type's `'cli'` value is renamed to `'patron'` across the codebase. 11 12 ## Current State 13 14 The guild has two interface surfaces: the `nsg` CLI and the MCP tool server. There is no web dashboard. 15 16 **`ToolCaller` type** in `packages/plugins/tools/src/tool.ts`: 17 18 ```typescript 19 export type ToolCaller = 'cli' | 'anima' | 'library'; 20 ``` 21 22 The `'cli'` value is used in `callableBy` declarations across ~19 files (CLI framework commands, animator/summon, tests). The CLI filters tools via: 23 24 ```typescript 25 // packages/framework/cli/src/program.ts line 175 26 .filter((r) => !r.definition.callableBy || r.definition.callableBy.includes('cli')) 27 ``` 28 29 **Kit contribution scanning** is established via the `consumes` declaration and the `plugin:initialized` startup event. The Instrumentarium consumes `'tools'`; the Loom consumes `'roles'`. No apparatus currently consumes `'pages'` or `'routes'`. 30 31 **Tool permission field** uses two formats in the codebase: simple levels (`'read'`, `'write'`, `'delete'`) and plugin-prefixed (`'clerk:read'`, `'clerk:write'`, `'spider:write'`). Custom levels like `'animate'` also exist. 32 33 **Query param coercion** in `packages/framework/cli/src/helpers.ts` handles number coercion only (via `isNumberSchema` + `coerceCliOpts`). Boolean coercion is not needed by the CLI (Commander handles boolean flags natively). 34 35 ## Requirements 36 37 - R1: The `ToolCaller` type must change from `'cli' | 'anima' | 'library'` to `'patron' | 'anima' | 'library'`. Every file referencing `'cli'` as a `ToolCaller` value must be updated. 38 - R2: A new package `@shardworks/oculus-apparatus` must be created at `packages/plugins/oculus/` following the established apparatus package conventions. 39 - R3: The Oculus apparatus must declare `requires: ['tools']` and `consumes: ['pages', 'routes']`. 40 - R4: The Oculus must start a Hono HTTP server in `start()`, awaiting port binding. The port must be configurable via `guild.json` under the `oculus` key (default: `7470`). It must log the listening URL at startup. 41 - R5: The Oculus must implement `stop()` to close the HTTP server. 42 - R6: The Oculus must scan page and route contributions from kits (via `g.kits()`), already-started apparatuses (via `g.apparatuses()`), and late-arriving apparatuses (via `ctx.on('plugin:initialized', ...)`). 43 - R7: Page contributions must specify `id`, `title`, and `dir` (relative to the package root). The Oculus must resolve `dir` to an absolute path via `node_modules/{packageName}/{dir}`. Pages must be served under `/pages/{id}/` by reading files from disk on each request. 44 - R8: When serving a page's `index.html` (and only `index.html`), the Oculus must inject a `` to `/static/style.css` before `` and a `
` element after ``. Pages without `` or `` tags must be served unmodified. 45 - R9: The nav must include a link to the home page (`/`) as the first item, followed by links to all registered pages. 46 - R10: The Oculus must serve its own static assets (stylesheet) from `/static/*`, resolved via `import.meta.dirname` relative to the source file. 47 - R11: The stylesheet must be a static CSS file providing CSS custom properties (Tokyo Night palette), element-type selectors for baseline styling, and utility classes for common patterns. 48 - R12: `GET /` must return a dynamically generated home page showing the guild name and a listing of all registered pages with links. The home page must include the stylesheet and nav directly in its HTML (no chrome injection pass). 49 - R13: The Oculus must auto-map patron-callable tools to REST endpoints. Tools are patron-callable when `callableBy` is absent or includes `'patron'`. 50 - R14: Tool names must be transformed to URL paths by splitting on the first hyphen only: `writ-list` becomes `/api/writ/list`; `signal` becomes `/api/signal`; `rig-for-writ` becomes `/api/rig/for-writ`. 51 - R15: HTTP method must be inferred from the tool's `permission` field. Extract the level (split on `:`, take the last segment). `'read'` or no permission maps to `GET`; `'write'` or `'admin'` maps to `POST`; `'delete'` maps to `DELETE`. Unknown levels default to `POST`. 52 - R16: GET endpoints must coerce query string parameters from strings to numbers and booleans based on the tool's Zod schema. POST/DELETE endpoints must parse a JSON request body. 53 - R17: Tool endpoint errors must return `400` for Zod validation failures (with `{ error: string, details: unknown }`) and `500` for handler errors (with `{ error: string }`). Successful responses return `200` with the handler result as JSON. 54 - R18: Tool routes must be registered at startup from `instrumentarium.list()`, with additional routes added for late-arriving tools via `plugin:initialized`. 55 - R19: Custom route contributions must specify `method`, `path`, and a Hono `Context` handler. The Oculus must reject routes whose path does not start with `/api/` (log warning, skip registration). 56 - R20: Custom routes must be registered before tool routes. When a tool route conflicts with a custom route path, the tool route must be skipped with a startup warning. 57 - R21: `GET /api/_tools` must return a JSON array of all auto-mapped tools, each with `name`, `route`, `method`, `description`, and `params` (using the `{ type, description, optional }` per-param format from tools-show). 58 - R22: The `OculusApi` provides object must expose `port(): number`. 59 60 ## Design 61 62 ### Type Changes 63 64 **`packages/plugins/tools/src/tool.ts`** — rename only: 65 66 ```typescript 67 export type ToolCaller = 'patron' | 'anima' | 'library'; 68 ``` 69 70 **`packages/plugins/oculus/src/types.ts`** — new file: 71 72 ```typescript 73 import type { Context } from 'hono'; 74 75 /** A page contributed by a plugin kit or apparatus supportKit. */ 76 export interface PageContribution { 77 /** Unique page ID — becomes the URL segment: /pages/{id}/ */ 78 id: string; 79 /** Human-readable title used in navigation. */ 80 title: string; 81 /** 82 * Path to the directory containing the page's static assets, 83 * relative to the contributing package's root in node_modules. 84 * Must contain an index.html entry point. 85 */ 86 dir: string; 87 } 88 89 /** A custom route contributed by a plugin kit or apparatus supportKit. */ 90 export interface RouteContribution { 91 /** HTTP method (uppercase): 'GET', 'POST', 'DELETE', etc. */ 92 method: string; 93 /** Hono path pattern. Must begin with /api/. */ 94 path: string; 95 /** Hono handler function. */ 96 handler: (c: Context) => Response | Promise; 97 } 98 99 /** Kit contribution interface — consumed by the Oculus. */ 100 export interface OculusKit { 101 pages?: PageContribution[]; 102 routes?: RouteContribution[]; 103 } 104 105 /** The Oculus configuration from guild.json under 'oculus'. */ 106 export interface OculusConfig { 107 /** Port to listen on. Default: 7470. */ 108 port?: number; 109 } 110 111 /** The Oculus's public API, exposed via provides. */ 112 export interface OculusApi { 113 /** The port the server is listening on. */ 114 port(): number; 115 } 116 ``` 117 118 **`packages/plugins/oculus/src/index.ts`** — barrel with GuildConfig augmentation: 119 120 ```typescript 121 import { createOculus } from './oculus.ts'; 122 123 export { 124 type OculusApi, 125 type OculusConfig, 126 type OculusKit, 127 type PageContribution, 128 type RouteContribution, 129 } from './types.ts'; 130 131 export { createOculus } from './oculus.ts'; 132 133 import type { OculusConfig } from './types.ts'; 134 declare module '@shardworks/nexus-core' { 135 interface GuildConfig { 136 oculus?: OculusConfig; 137 } 138 } 139 140 export default createOculus(); 141 ``` 142 143 ### Behavior 144 145 #### ToolCaller Rename (S1) 146 147 Every literal `'cli'` used as a `ToolCaller` value becomes `'patron'`. This is a mechanical find-and-replace scoped to ToolCaller contexts. The complete file list: 148 149 **Type definition:** 150 - `packages/plugins/tools/src/tool.ts` — the `ToolCaller` type union 151 152 **Production code using `callableBy: 'cli'` or `callableBy: ['cli']`:** 153 - `packages/framework/cli/src/program.ts` — `.includes('cli')` filter 154 - `packages/framework/cli/src/cli.ts` — JSDoc comment mentioning `'cli'` 155 - `packages/framework/cli/src/commands/status.ts` 156 - `packages/framework/cli/src/commands/init.ts` 157 - `packages/framework/cli/src/commands/version.ts` 158 - `packages/framework/cli/src/commands/upgrade.ts` 159 - `packages/framework/cli/src/commands/plugin.ts` (4 tool definitions) 160 - `packages/plugins/animator/src/tools/summon.ts` 161 162 **Zod enum in tools-list:** 163 - `packages/plugins/tools/src/tools/tools-list.ts` — `z.enum(['cli', ...])` 164 165 **Test files (update string literals and assertions):** 166 - `packages/plugins/tools/src/tool.test.ts` 167 - `packages/plugins/tools/src/instrumentarium.test.ts` 168 - `packages/plugins/tools/src/tools/tools-list.test.ts` 169 - `packages/plugins/tools/src/tools/tools-show.test.ts` 170 - `packages/plugins/claude-code/src/mcp-server.test.ts` 171 - `packages/framework/cli/src/commands/version.test.ts` 172 - `packages/framework/cli/src/commands/plugin.test.ts` 173 - `packages/framework/cli/src/commands/status.test.ts` 174 - `packages/framework/cli/src/commands/upgrade.test.ts` 175 176 #### Apparatus Lifecycle (S2) 177 178 `createOculus()` returns a `Plugin` with an `apparatus` that: 179 180 1. Declares `requires: ['tools']`, `consumes: ['pages', 'routes']`, `provides: api`. 181 2. In `start()`: 182 - Reads config: `const oculusConfig = guild().guildConfig().oculus ?? {}; const port = oculusConfig.port ?? 7470;` 183 - Creates a `Hono` instance. 184 - Scans contributions (pages + routes) from `g.kits()`, `g.apparatuses()`, and subscribes to `plugin:initialized` for late arrivals. Page `dir` is resolved to absolute path: `path.join(guild().home, 'node_modules', plugin.packageName, contribution.dir)`. 185 - Registers custom routes first (validating `/api/` prefix — warn and skip invalid ones). 186 - Gets `InstrumentariumApi` via `guild().apparatus('tools')`, filters to patron-callable tools, registers tool routes (skipping conflicts with custom route paths, warning on conflict). 187 - Registers `GET /api/_tools` endpoint. 188 - Registers page-serving routes for each page: `/pages/{id}/*`. 189 - Registers `/static/*` route for own assets. 190 - Registers `GET /` for the home page. 191 - Starts the server via `serve({ fetch: app.fetch, port })` and awaits the listen callback wrapping it in a Promise. Logs `[oculus] Listening on http://localhost:{port}`. 192 - Updates the `api.port()` return value. 193 3. In `stop()`: closes the HTTP server via `server.close()`. 194 4. In `plugin:initialized` handler: scans late-arriving apparatus supportKit for pages and routes. For pages, registers new `/pages/{id}/*` routes. For routes, registers new custom routes. For new tools arriving via the Instrumentarium (query `instrumentarium.list()` for tools not yet mapped), registers new tool routes. 195 196 #### Page Serving (S2, S3) 197 198 When a request arrives for `/pages/{pageId}/*`: 199 200 1. Resolve the requested file path within the page's resolved directory. Prevent directory traversal (reject paths containing `..`). 201 2. If the file is `index.html` (the request path is `/pages/{id}/` or `/pages/{id}/index.html`): 202 - Read the file from disk. 203 - Apply chrome injection (R8): case-insensitive regex to find `` and ``, insert nav HTML after ``. If either tag is absent, serve as-is. 204 - Return with `Content-Type: text/html`. 205 3. For all other files: read from disk, determine Content-Type from extension, return. 206 4. If file not found: return 404. 207 208 **Content-Type lookup table** (built into the apparatus): 209 210 ```typescript 211 const MIME_TYPES: Record = { 212 '.html': 'text/html; charset=utf-8', 213 '.css': 'text/css; charset=utf-8', 214 '.js': 'application/javascript; charset=utf-8', 215 '.mjs': 'application/javascript; charset=utf-8', 216 '.json': 'application/json; charset=utf-8', 217 '.png': 'image/png', 218 '.jpg': 'image/jpeg', 219 '.jpeg': 'image/jpeg', 220 '.gif': 'image/gif', 221 '.svg': 'image/svg+xml', 222 '.ico': 'image/x-icon', 223 '.woff': 'font/woff', 224 '.woff2': 'font/woff2', 225 '.ttf': 'font/ttf', 226 '.map': 'application/json', 227 }; 228 // Default: 'application/octet-stream' 229 ``` 230 231 #### Chrome Injection (S3) 232 233 `injectChrome(html: string, stylesheetPath: string, navHtml: string): string` 234 235 1. Find `` (case-insensitive). If found, insert `` before it. 236 2. Find `` (case-insensitive, tolerant of attributes). If found, insert `navHtml` after the closing `>` of the body tag. 237 3. If neither tag is found, return html unmodified. 238 239 The nav HTML is generated once at startup (and updated when late pages arrive): 240 241 ```html 242
243 Guild 244 {title} 245 246
247 ``` 248 249 #### Shared Stylesheet (S3) 250 251 File: `packages/plugins/oculus/src/static/style.css` 252 253 Served at `/static/style.css`. Three layers: 254 255 **Layer 1 — Custom properties:** 256 ```css 257 :root { 258 --bg: #1a1b26; 259 --surface: #24283b; 260 --surface2: #2f3549; 261 --border: #3b4261; 262 --text: #c0caf5; 263 --text-dim: #565f89; 264 --text-bright: #e0e6ff; 265 --green: #9ece6a; 266 --red: #f7768e; 267 --yellow: #e0af68; 268 --cyan: #7dcfff; 269 --magenta: #bb9af7; 270 --blue: #7aa2f7; 271 --font-mono: "SF Mono", "Fira Code", "JetBrains Mono", monospace; 272 } 273 ``` 274 275 **Layer 2 — Element-type selectors:** 276 - `body` — `background: var(--bg); color: var(--text); font-family: var(--font-mono); font-size: 13px; line-height: 1.6; margin: 0; padding: 0;` 277 - `h1, h2, h3, h4` — `color: var(--text-bright); font-weight: 600;` 278 - `a` — `color: var(--cyan); text-decoration: none;` with hover underline 279 - `table` — `width: 100%; border-collapse: collapse;` 280 - `th, td` — `text-align: left; padding: 8px; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border);` 281 - `th` — `color: var(--text-dim); font-weight: 500;` 282 - `button` — solid accent color, dark text, rounded corners (6px), 8px 16px padding 283 - `input, select, textarea` — dark background (`var(--surface)`), border `var(--border)`, focus border `var(--cyan)` 284 - `pre, code` — `background: var(--surface); border-radius: 4px;` — `code` inline gets small padding; `pre` block gets `padding: 16px; overflow-x: auto;` 285 286 **Layer 3 — Utility classes:** 287 - `#oculus-nav` — `display: flex; gap: 16px; align-items: center; padding: 8px 16px; background: var(--surface); border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border);` 288 - `#oculus-nav a` — `color: var(--text-dim); font-size: 12px;` with active/hover → `color: var(--text)` 289 - `.card` — `background: var(--surface); border: 1px solid var(--border); border-radius: 8px; padding: 16px;` 290 - `.badge` — `display: inline-block; font-size: 11px; padding: 2px 8px; border-radius: 4px; background: var(--surface2); color: var(--text-dim);` 291 - `.badge--success` — `background: rgba(158,206,106,0.15); color: var(--green);` 292 - `.badge--error` — `background: rgba(247,118,142,0.15); color: var(--red);` 293 - `.badge--warning` — `background: rgba(224,175,104,0.15); color: var(--yellow);` 294 - `.badge--info` — `background: rgba(125,207,255,0.15); color: var(--cyan);` 295 - `.badge--active` — same as `--info` plus a pulsing animation 296 - `.data-table` — inherits base table styles, with alternating row tint 297 - `.btn` — base button reset with cursor pointer 298 - `.btn--primary` — `background: var(--blue); color: var(--bg);` 299 - `.btn--success` — `background: var(--green); color: var(--bg);` 300 - `.btn--danger` — `background: var(--red); color: var(--bg);` 301 - `.toolbar` — `display: flex; gap: 8px; align-items: center; padding: 8px 0;` 302 - `.empty-state` — `text-align: center; padding: 48px 16px; color: var(--text-dim);` 303 - `@keyframes pulse` — subtle opacity pulse (`1 → 0.6 → 1` over 2s) applied to `.badge--active` 304 305 #### Tool→REST Auto-Mapping (S4) 306 307 **`toolNameToRoute(name: string): string`** — split on first hyphen: 308 ```typescript 309 function toolNameToRoute(name: string): string { 310 const idx = name.indexOf('-'); 311 if (idx === -1) return `/api/${name}`; 312 return `/api/${name.slice(0, idx)}/${name.slice(idx + 1)}`; 313 } 314 ``` 315 316 **`permissionToMethod(permission: string | undefined): string`** — extract level, map: 317 ```typescript 318 function permissionToMethod(permission: string | undefined): 'GET' | 'POST' | 'DELETE' { 319 if (permission === undefined) return 'GET'; 320 const level = permission.includes(':') ? permission.slice(permission.lastIndexOf(':') + 1) : permission; 321 if (level === 'read') return 'GET'; 322 if (level === 'write' || level === 'admin') return 'POST'; 323 if (level === 'delete') return 'DELETE'; 324 return 'POST'; // unknown levels default to POST 325 } 326 ``` 327 328 **Tool route handler** — for each tool: 329 ```typescript 330 // For GET: 331 app.get(routePath, async (c) => { 332 const rawQuery = c.req.query(); // Record 333 const coerced = coerceParams(shape, rawQuery); 334 const validated = toolDef.params.parse(coerced); 335 const result = await toolDef.handler(validated); 336 return c.json(result); 337 }); 338 339 // For POST/DELETE: 340 app.post(routePath, async (c) => { // or app.delete() 341 const body = await c.req.json(); 342 const validated = toolDef.params.parse(body); 343 const result = await toolDef.handler(validated); 344 return c.json(result); 345 }); 346 ``` 347 348 Error handling wraps each handler: 349 - Zod parse failure → `c.json({ error: err.message, details: err.issues }, 400)` 350 - Handler exception → `c.json({ error: message }, 500)` 351 352 **`coerceParams(shape, params): Record`** — extends the CLI's coercion with boolean support: 353 ```typescript 354 function coerceParams( 355 shape: Record, 356 params: Record, 357 ): Record { 358 const result: Record = { ...params }; 359 for (const [key, schema] of Object.entries(shape)) { 360 const value = result[key]; 361 if (typeof value !== 'string') continue; 362 if (isNumberSchema(schema)) { 363 result[key] = Number(value); 364 } else if (isBooleanSchema(schema)) { 365 result[key] = value === 'true'; 366 } 367 } 368 return result; 369 } 370 ``` 371 372 Where `isNumberSchema` and `isBooleanSchema` replicate the pattern from `packages/framework/cli/src/helpers.ts` — unwrap `ZodOptional`/`ZodDefault`, then check the inner type with `instanceof z.ZodNumber` / `instanceof z.ZodBoolean`. 373 374 #### Custom Route Contributions (S5) 375 376 During contribution scanning, for each `route` in a kit's `routes` array: 377 1. Check `route.path.startsWith('/api/')`. If not, log `[oculus] Custom route "${route.path}" from "${pluginId}" must start with /api/ — skipped` and skip. 378 2. Register the route on the Hono app: `app[route.method.toLowerCase()](route.path, route.handler)`. 379 3. Add `route.path` to a `Set` of custom route paths. 380 381 When registering tool routes, check each computed tool route path against this Set. If it's present, log `[oculus] Tool route ${method} ${path} conflicts with custom route from plugin — skipped` and skip the tool route. 382 383 #### API Tool Index (S6) 384 385 `GET /api/_tools` handler returns a JSON array: 386 387 ```typescript 388 interface ToolIndexEntry { 389 name: string; 390 route: string; 391 method: 'GET' | 'POST' | 'DELETE'; 392 description: string; 393 params: Record; 394 } 395 ``` 396 397 The params extraction replicates the `extractParams`/`extractSingleParam`/`zodTypeToJsonType` logic from `packages/plugins/tools/src/tools/tools-show.ts`. This is reimplemented locally (not imported, since tools-show doesn't export these functions). 398 399 #### Home Page (S7) 400 401 `GET /` returns a dynamically generated HTML string with: 402 - The stylesheet linked directly (``) 403 - The nav included directly (same HTML as injected chrome) 404 - The guild name from `guild().guildConfig().name` in an `

` 405 - A listing of registered pages, each as a link to `/pages/{id}/` with the page title 406 407 No chrome injection pass needed — the home page includes everything directly. 408 409 ### Non-obvious Touchpoints 410 411 - `packages/plugins/tools/src/tools/tools-list.ts` — the Zod `z.enum` for the `caller` param must change from `['cli', 'anima', 'library']` to `['patron', 'anima', 'library']`. 412 - `packages/plugins/animator/src/tools/summon.ts` — uses `callableBy: 'cli'` which must become `'patron'`. 413 - `packages/plugins/claude-code/src/mcp-server.test.ts` — test fixtures reference `'cli'` as a ToolCaller value. 414 - `packages/framework/cli/src/commands/plugin.ts` — has 4 separate tool definitions each with `callableBy: ['cli']`. 415 - `pnpm-workspace.yaml` already includes `packages/plugins/*` so the new oculus package is automatically a workspace member. 416 417 ### Dependencies 418 419 The `'cli'` → `'patron'` rename (S1) is a prerequisite for the Oculus apparatus. The Oculus filters tools with `caller: 'patron'`; if the rename hasn't happened, no tools will match. commit dfac45361577a8887cc3cb2dc8ec6289104102c5
Author: Artificer <w-mnorf3k0-51b5791bdbdd@nexus.local>
Date:   Tue Apr 7 15:45:09 2026 +0000

    Add Oculus web dashboard apparatus and rename ToolCaller 'cli' to 'patron'
    
    Renames the ToolCaller value 'cli' to 'patron' across 19 files (source +
    tests) to better reflect the human patron/operator role vs the CLI surface.
    
    Adds @shardworks/oculus-apparatus at packages/plugins/oculus — a Hono-based
    HTTP dashboard that:
    - Auto-maps patron-callable guild tools to REST endpoints (GET/POST/DELETE
      based on permission level)
    - Serves contributed pages as static asset directories with shared chrome
      injection (Tokyo Night stylesheet + nav bar)
    - Supports custom /api/* route contributions from kits and apparatus supportKits
    - Exposes GET /api/_tools for tool discovery
    - Serves own static assets from /static/*
    - Renders a dynamic home page at GET /
    
    Includes 44 unit and integration tests covering all major behaviors.

Author: Artificer <w-mnorf3k0-51b5791bdbdd@nexus.local>

    Add Oculus web dashboard apparatus and rename ToolCaller 'cli' to 'patron'

diff --git a/packages/plugins/oculus/package.json b/packages/plugins/oculus/package.json
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..56e9107
--- /dev/null
+++ b/packages/plugins/oculus/package.json
@@ -0,0 +1,41 @@
+{
+  "name": "@shardworks/oculus-apparatus",
+  "version": "0.0.0",
+  "license": "ISC",
+  "repository": {
+    "type": "git",
+    "url": "https://github.com/shardworks/nexus",
+    "directory": "packages/plugins/oculus"
+  },
+  "description": "The Oculus — web dashboard apparatus for the guild",
+  "type": "module",
+  "exports": {
+    ".": "./src/index.ts"
+  },
+  "scripts": {
+    "build": "tsc",
+    "test": "node --disable-warning=ExperimentalWarning --experimental-transform-types --test 'src/**/*.test.ts'",
+    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit"
+  },
+  "dependencies": {
+    "@hono/node-server": "^1.13.7",
+    "@shardworks/nexus-core": "workspace:*",
+    "@shardworks/tools-apparatus": "workspace:*",
+    "hono": "^4.7.11",
+    "zod": "4.3.6"
+  },
+  "devDependencies": {
+    "@types/node": "25.5.0"
+  },
+  "files": [
+    "dist"
+  ],
+  "publishConfig": {
+    "exports": {
+      ".": {
+        "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
+        "import": "./dist/index.js"
+      }
+    }
+  }
+}
diff --git a/packages/plugins/oculus/src/index.ts b/packages/plugins/oculus/src/index.ts
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..2f3c5ac
--- /dev/null
+++ b/packages/plugins/oculus/src/index.ts
@@ -0,0 +1,28 @@
+/**
+ * @shardworks/oculus-apparatus — The Oculus.
+ *
+ * Web dashboard apparatus for the guild. Serves pages contributed by plugins,
+ * exposes guild tools as REST endpoints, and provides a unified web interface.
+ */
+
+import { createOculus } from './oculus.ts';
+
+export {
+  type OculusApi,
+  type OculusConfig,
+  type OculusKit,
+  type PageContribution,
+  type RouteContribution,
+} from './types.ts';
+
+export { createOculus } from './oculus.ts';
+
+import type { OculusConfig } from './types.ts';
+
+declare module '@shardworks/nexus-core' {
+  interface GuildConfig {
+    oculus?: OculusConfig;
+  }
+}
+
+export default createOculus();
diff --git a/packages/plugins/oculus/src/types.ts b/packages/plugins/oculus/src/types.ts
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..7255f22
--- /dev/null
+++ b/packages/plugins/oculus/src/types.ts
@@ -0,0 +1,43 @@
+import type { Context } from 'hono';
+
+/** A page contributed by a plugin kit or apparatus supportKit. */
+export interface PageContribution {
+  /** Unique page ID — becomes the URL segment: /pages/{id}/ */
+  id: string;
+  /** Human-readable title used in navigation. */
+  title: string;
+  /**
+   * Path to the directory containing the page's static assets,
+   * relative to the contributing package's root in node_modules.
+   * Must contain an index.html entry point.
+   */
+  dir: string;
+}
+
+/** A custom route contributed by a plugin kit or apparatus supportKit. */
+export interface RouteContribution {
+  /** HTTP method (uppercase): 'GET', 'POST', 'DELETE', etc. */
+  method: string;
+  /** Hono path pattern. Must begin with /api/. */
+  path: string;
+  /** Hono handler function. */
+  handler: (c: Context) => Response | Promise<Response>;
+}
+
+/** Kit contribution interface — consumed by the Oculus. */
+export interface OculusKit {

commit dfac45361577a8887cc3cb2dc8ec6289104102c5
Author: Artificer <w-mnorf3k0-51b5791bdbdd@nexus.local>
Date:   Tue Apr 7 15:45:09 2026 +0000

    Add Oculus web dashboard apparatus and rename ToolCaller 'cli' to 'patron'
    
    Renames the ToolCaller value 'cli' to 'patron' across 19 files (source +
    tests) to better reflect the human patron/operator role vs the CLI surface.
    
    Adds @shardworks/oculus-apparatus at packages/plugins/oculus — a Hono-based
    HTTP dashboard that:
    - Auto-maps patron-callable guild tools to REST endpoints (GET/POST/DELETE
      based on permission level)
    - Serves contributed pages as static asset directories with shared chrome
      injection (Tokyo Night stylesheet + nav bar)
    - Supports custom /api/* route contributions from kits and apparatus supportKits
    - Exposes GET /api/_tools for tool discovery
    - Serves own static assets from /static/*
    - Renders a dynamic home page at GET /
    
    Includes 44 unit and integration tests covering all major behaviors.

+/**
+ * @shardworks/oculus-apparatus — The Oculus.
+ *
+ * Web dashboard apparatus for the guild. Serves pages contributed by plugins,
+ * exposes guild tools as REST endpoints, and provides a unified web interface.
+ */
+
+import { createOculus } from './oculus.ts';
+
+export {
+  type OculusApi,
+  type OculusConfig,
+  type OculusKit,
+  type PageContribution,
+  type RouteContribution,
+} from './types.ts';
+
+export { createOculus } from './oculus.ts';
+
+import type { OculusConfig } from './types.ts';
+
+declare module '@shardworks/nexus-core' {
+  interface GuildConfig {
+    oculus?: OculusConfig;
+  }
+}
+
+export default createOculus();

new file mode 100644

@@ -0,0 +1,43 @@
+import type { Context } from 'hono';
+
+/** A page contributed by a plugin kit or apparatus supportKit. */
+export interface PageContribution {
+  /** Unique page ID — becomes the URL segment: /pages/{id}/ */
+  id: string;
+  /** Human-readable title used in navigation. */
+  title: string;
+  /**
+   * Path to the directory containing the page's static assets,
+   * relative to the contributing package's root in node_modules.
+   * Must contain an index.html entry point.
+   */
+  dir: string;
+}
+
+/** A custom route contributed by a plugin kit or apparatus supportKit. */
+export interface RouteContribution {
+  /** HTTP method (uppercase): 'GET', 'POST', 'DELETE', etc. */
+  method: string;
+  /** Hono path pattern. Must begin with /api/. */
+  path: string;
+  /** Hono handler function. */
+  handler: (c: Context) => Response | Promise<Response>;
+}
+
+/** Kit contribution interface — consumed by the Oculus. */
+export interface OculusKit {
+  pages?: PageContribution[];
+  routes?: RouteContribution[];
+}
+
+/** The Oculus configuration from guild.json under 'oculus'. */
+export interface OculusConfig {
+  /** Port to listen on. Default: 7470. */
+  port?: number;
+}
+
+/** The Oculus's public API, exposed via provides. */
+export interface OculusApi {
+  /** The port the server is listening on. */
+  port(): number;
+}

content.text:
Alright Sean, I've given this a thorough going-over. Here's the verdict:

---

## The Oculus — w-mnorf3k0-51b5791bdbdd

**Cost:** $6.79 total across 3 sessions (implement: $6.48 / 31min, review: $0.25 / 2min, revise: $0.06 / 13sec)

### The Good

This is **remarkably clean** for a complexity 20 commission. The last time we tried a web dashboard (w-mni87qen, also cx 20, weak spec), we got a broken monolith with fatal JS syntax errors. This time:

- **Typecheck clean** — `pnpm -r typecheck` passes across all packages, zero errors
- **44 tests, all passing** — unit tests for every helper function (toolNameToRoute, permissionToMethod, coerceParams, injectChrome), plus real integration tests that spin up actual HTTP servers and make fetch requests. Good test architecture.
- **ToolCaller rename executed perfectly** — all 19 files updated, no residual `'cli'` in ToolCaller contexts, existing test suites all pass with the new values
- **Follows spec faithfully** — types.ts is essentially verbatim from the spec, oculus.ts implements all 22 requirements, the architecture doc was removed (since it was pre-committed by you)
- **No monolith problem** — 537 lines of implementation in oculus.ts is reasonable for the scope. Clean separation: types.ts (43 lines), index.ts (28 lines), oculus.ts (537), style.css (225), tests (767)
- **The revise session was a 13-second no-op** — reviewer found nothing to fix. Second time we've seen this pattern with a strong spec (Copilot was the first).

### Things Worth Noting

1. **Handler code duplication** — the GET/POST/DELETE route handlers each have identical error handling (catch ZodError → 400, catch Error → 500). That's ~15 lines copy-pasted three times. A shared `wrapHandler` would reduce this, but it's not wrong, just verbose.

2. **`void instrumentarium;`** — there's a `void instrumentarium;` line in `registerToolRoute` to suppress an unused warning. The parameter was probably intended for the `_tools` endpoint or late-arriving tool detection, but it's passed in and then voided. Minor smell.

3. **`readFileSync` in request handlers** — page serving and static asset serving use synchronous fs reads per request. Fine for a dev dashboard, but worth knowing it's not production-grade.

4. **No custom route conflict test** — V11 from the validation checklist (tool route conflicts with custom route at the same path) isn't explicitly tested. The custom route tests cover *registration* but don't test the conflict-skip behavior with a competing tool route.

5. **`ZodLiteral` type extraction** — the reimplemented `zodTypeToJsonType` uses `zodType._def.values[0]` for `ZodLiteral`, which looks like it's reaching into Zod internals. This could break on a Zod version bump. (The original tools-show has the same issue, so this is systemic, not Oculus-specific.)

6. **Home page XSS** — guild name and page titles are interpolated directly into HTML template strings with no escaping. If a guild name contains `<script>`, it'll execute. Dev tool, so low risk, but worth flagging.

### Comparison with the First Dashboard Attempt

| | w-mni87qen (Mk 1) | w-mnorf3k0 (Oculus) |
|---|---|---|
| Complexity | 20 | 20 |
| Spec quality | weak | strong |
| Outcome | partial/broken | success |
| Lines of code | ~1000 monolith | 537 + 225 CSS |
| Tests | 0 | 44 |
| Fatal bugs | JS syntax error | None |
| Cost | — | $6.79 |

The difference between weak spec → broken and strong spec → clean is *very* pronounced here. Same complexity, same domain, completely different result.

### My Assessment

**Outcome: success.** All 22 requirements met, typecheck clean, tests pass, no bugs found. The spec did the heavy lifting — this is the Copilot pattern again where a detailed spec reduces the anima's job to near-transcription, and the result is correspondingly reliable.

Want me to record this in the review and update the commission log?

# Review: w-mnorf3k0-51b5791bdbdd

## The Oculus — Web Dashboard Apparatus

**Outcome:** success

**Spec quality (post-review):** strong

**Revision required:** no

## Notes

All 22 requirements met. Typecheck clean across all packages. 44 tests passing —
good mix of unit tests (helpers) and integration tests (real HTTP servers with fetch).
ToolCaller rename executed perfectly across 19 files with no residual `'cli'` references.

The revise session was a 13-second no-op ($0.06) — reviewer found nothing to fix.
Second instance of this pattern with a strong, detailed spec (after Copilot w-mnolvtcc).

Notable observations:
- Handler error logic (ZodError→400, Error→500) is copy-pasted across GET/POST/DELETE
  branches rather than extracted into a shared wrapper. Not wrong, just verbose.
- `readFileSync` in request handlers — fine for a dev dashboard, not production-grade.
- V11 (tool route conflict with custom route) from the validation checklist is not
  explicitly tested.
- Guild name and page titles interpolated into HTML with no escaping (XSS vector,
  low risk for a dev tool).
- No startup tool or CLI command — the apparatus exists but there's no mechanism to
  actually start it. Spec didn't include one.

Compare with first dashboard attempt (w-mni87qen, also cx 20, weak spec): broken
monolith with fatal JS syntax error, 0 tests. Same complexity, strong spec → clean
delivery. The spec-quality signal is very strong at this complexity level.

Total cost: $6.79 across 3 sessions (implement $6.48, review $0.25, revise $0.06).

# Commission Log — Standing Research Instrument
#
# One entry per patron-sourced commission. Patron-subjective judgments only.
# Full artifacts live in experiments/data/commissions/<id>/ — see README there.
#
# IDs are writ IDs (w-*) from the Clerk. Pre-Clerk commissions used session
# IDs (ses-*) or ad-hoc labels — these are grandfathered with a note field.
#
# Fields:
#   id                — writ ID (canonical) or legacy session/label ID
#   title             — short description of the commissioned work
#   codex             — target codex name
#   complexity        — patron estimate (Fibonacci: 1 2 3 5 8 13 21)
#   spec_quality_pre  — patron assessment of spec before dispatch
#   outcome           — patron assessment after review
#   revision_required — whether follow-up work was needed
#   spec_quality_post — patron assessment of spec after review
#   reviewed_at       — ISO date when patron reviewed this commission (null = not reviewed)
#                       Backfill note: all commissions through w-mnjp1ph3 were reviewed by
#                       the patron (with Coco) on the dates shown. Dates are approximate,
#                       derived from review-related commit timestamps. After w-mnjp1ph3,
#                       patron review became ad hoc — the planning workshop and static
#                       implement→review→revise rig structure reduced perceived need.
#   failure_mode      — if outcome != success, what went wrong (omit if n/a)
#   note              — meta info about the record itself (not content)
#
# Scales:
#   Complexity (Fibonacci): 1 2 3 5 8 13 21
#   Spec quality: strong | adequate | weak
#   Outcome: success | partial | wrong | abandoned
#   Failure mode: spec_ambiguous | requirement_wrong | execution_error | complexity_overrun | broken | incomplete

# Coco Log — Standing Research Instrument
#
# One entry per unit of work handled directly by Coco (the interactive
# collaborator) rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission.
# Parallel to the commission log — together they capture the full
# picture of how work is routed in the system.
#
# An entry is created for each commit (or tight cluster of commits
# toward a single goal) that Coco produces during a session.
#
# Fields:
#   session        — Coco's Claude session ID (join key to archived transcripts)
#   date           — date the work was done (YYYY-MM-DD)
#   item           — short description of the work (one line)
#   commissionable — whether this work could have been an autonomous commission
#   justification  — why it can't be commissioned (required when commissionable is false)
#
# Justifications (required when commissionable: false):
#   interactive     — required real-time iteration with Sean
#   sanctum         — sanctum/experiment/meta work outside the commission boundary
#   decisional      — output was a decision, not an artifact

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# instrument-review.sh — Run instrument suite against a commission.
#
# Runs quality scorers (blind + aware) and optionally the codebase
# integration scorer via the generic instrument runner.
#
# Usage:
#   ./bin/instrument-review.sh --commission <id> --repo <path> [options]
#
# Options:
#   --commission <id>    Commission/writ ID (required)
#   --repo <path>        Path to git repo (required)
#   --spec-file <path>   Commission spec (enables aware + integration scoring)
#   --base-commit <sha>  Override: start of commit range
#   --commit <sha>       Override: end of commit range
#   --output-dir <path>  Where to write artifacts
#   --dry-run            Print plan without executing
#   --blind-only         Only run spec-blind quality scorer
#   --aware-only         Only run spec-aware quality scorer
#   --integration-only   Only run codebase integration scorer
#   --quality-only       Run both quality scorers, skip integration
#   --no-integration     Skip integration scorer (default: run if spec provided)

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
RUNNER="$PROJECT_ROOT/packages/instruments/src/cli.ts"

# ── Parse arguments ──────────────────────────────────────────

COMMISSION=""
REPO=""
SPEC_FILE=""
BASE_COMMIT=""
COMMIT=""
OUTPUT_DIR=""
DRY_RUN=""
BLIND_ONLY=false
AWARE_ONLY=false

# X013 — Commission Outcomes

## Research Question

What predicts good outcomes from autonomous commissions — and can
tracking outcomes over time serve as a health indicator for the system?

## Background

The system has been running autonomous commissions since 2026-03-25.
Failures occur, sometimes critically. But the attribution is unclear:
when a commission produces a wrong result, is that a spec quality
problem (patron's craft), a complexity problem (system capability
limit), or an agent deficiency (model or curriculum)? These three
causes have different fixes. Without separating them, every failure
is ambiguous and every improvement effort is a guess.

This experiment creates the tracking infrastructure to answer that
question over time — and tests whether revision rate (the fraction
of commissions requiring follow-up work) functions as a meaningful
health indicator for the system as it matures.

## Hypotheses

### H1 — Spec Quality Predicts Output Quality

Commission spec quality is a significant predictor of output quality,
independent of task complexity. A well-written spec — clear scope
boundaries, explicit event flows, unambiguous acceptance criteria —
produces better outcomes regardless of task complexity.

Output quality is measured on two axes:

- **Requirement satisfaction** (`outcome`) — did the commission meet
  its requirements? Measured as success / partial / wrong / abandoned.
- **Code quality** (`code_quality_agent`) — is the code well-written
  independent of requirements? Measured by the autonomous quality
  scorer (see Data Collection — Quality Scorer). This is a composite
  score (1.0–3.0) averaged across multiple independent reviewer runs,
  covering test quality, code structure, error handling, and codebase
  consistency.

Spec quality is defined against objective, agent-independent criteria
(see Data Collection). The same spec may produce different results
with different agents; that variation is agent deficiency, not spec
quality variation.

**If true:** Commission writing is a learnable craft that meaningfully
improves outcomes. Two levers exist: patron skill development (better
spec-writing practice, templates, checklists) and system-level
correction (agentic preparation — e.g., Sage consultation — that
identifies and repairs deficient specs before dispatch). These are
complementary: patron skill sets the baseline; agentic correction
catches what remains.

**If false:** Spec quality is a weak predictor. The bottleneck is

4	# X014 — Technical Spec Quality
5	
6	## Research Question
7	
8	Do commission specs with higher levels of technical implementation
9	detail produce better outcomes than mountain-quality specs on
10	comparable tasks — and if so, at what complexity threshold does
11	the difference emerge?
12	
13	## Background
14	
15	X013 defines spec quality in terms of mountain-spec adequacy:
16	clear scope, recognizable success criteria, product-level edge
17	cases. It explicitly does not reward technical implementation
18	detail — consistent with the system philosophy that patrons point
19	at mountains, not trails.
20	
21	X014 tests whether that philosophy holds empirically. If mountain-
22	quality specs produce outcomes as good as technically detailed specs,
23	the philosophy is validated. If technically detailed specs
24	consistently outperform them — particularly at higher complexity —
25	that is a signal the system needs more scaffolding than the design
26	currently assumes.
27	
28	## Methodology Note
29	
30	This experiment requires a **designed intervention**, not passive
31	observation. If commissions are consistently written as mountain-specs
32	(as the philosophy prescribes), there will be insufficient variance
33	in technical detail level to detect a relationship. The experiment
34	needs comparable tasks dispatched with deliberately varied levels of
35	implementation specificity.
36	
37	Technical detail level can be rated retroactively from spec text —
38	it is a property of the document and is not outcome-contaminated
39	the way mountain-quality ratings are. Existing commissions from
40	X013 can be backfilled.
41	
42	## Depends On
43	
44	- X013 (commission log infrastructure, outcome tracking, assessment
45	  tooling) — X014 extends the same corpus with an additional rating
46	  dimension
47	
[result-id: r39]

# X014 — Technical Spec Quality

## Research Question

Do commission specs with higher levels of technical implementation
detail produce better outcomes than mountain-quality specs on
comparable tasks — and if so, at what complexity threshold does
the difference emerge?

## Background

X013 defines spec quality in terms of mountain-spec adequacy:
clear scope, recognizable success criteria, product-level edge
cases. It explicitly does not reward technical implementation
detail — consistent with the system philosophy that patrons point
at mountains, not trails.

X014 tests whether that philosophy holds empirically. If mountain-
quality specs produce outcomes as good as technically detailed specs,
the philosophy is validated. If technically detailed specs
consistently outperform them — particularly at higher complexity —
that is a signal the system needs more scaffolding than the design
currently assumes.

## Methodology Note

This experiment requires a **designed intervention**, not passive
observation. If commissions are consistently written as mountain-specs
(as the philosophy prescribes), there will be insufficient variance
in technical detail level to detect a relationship. The experiment
needs comparable tasks dispatched with deliberately varied levels of
implementation specificity.

Technical detail level can be rated retroactively from spec text —
it is a property of the document and is not outcome-contaminated
the way mountain-quality ratings are. Existing commissions from
X013 can be backfilled.

## Depends On

- X013 (commission log infrastructure, outcome tracking, assessment
  tooling) — X014 extends the same corpus with an additional rating
  dimension

content.text:
Okay, here's the full picture. Let me bring this all together.

---

## Instrument Scores

| Scorer | Composite | σ | Highlights |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Blind** | 4.33 | 0.12 | codebase_consistency: 5.00, code_structure: 4.00, error_handling: 4.00 |
| **Aware** | 4.67 | 0.25 | requirement_coverage: 5.00, codebase_consistency: 5.00, one run gave a perfect 5.00 |
| **Integration** | 4.17 | 0.24 | scope_discipline: 5.00, utility_reuse: 3.33 |

**The interesting ding:** `utility_reuse: 3.33` — all three integration runs flagged the same thing I did: the `isNumberSchema`/`isBooleanSchema` coercion logic is reimplemented locally instead of importing from the CLI helpers. Two runs called it out explicitly. The `extractParams`/`zodTypeToJsonType` reimplementation from tools-show got mentioned too. The spec *told* the anima to reimplement these ("reimplemented locally, not imported, since tools-show doesn't export these functions"), so the anima followed instructions faithfully — but the scorers see duplicated utility logic and ding for it. That's actually a spec design choice, not an execution failure.

Everything else looks solid. The `scope_discipline: 5.00` across all three runs is notable — this anima stayed completely in lane, no scope creep.

## Followup Assessment

Looking at what I found and what the scorers found:

1. **Handler error duplication** (GET/POST/DELETE copy-paste) — the scorers noticed this under `code_structure: 4.00`. It's a minor refactor, but probably not worth a dedicated commission. If someone's in the Oculus for another reason, it's a "while you're here" fix.

2. **Utility reuse** (coercion logic, param extraction) — this is a real architectural question. The CLI helpers don't export their coercion functions, and tools-show doesn't export `extractParams`. Fixing this means modifying those packages to export utilities, then importing in Oculus. That's a proper commission — cross-package refactor with API surface changes.

3. **XSS / `readFileSync`** — dev dashboard, low risk. Not worth a followup unless the Oculus starts being used in production contexts.

4. **No startup mechanism** — this is the real gap. The apparatus *exists* but there's no way to actually start it. The `export default createOculus()` means it'll self-register when imported as a plugin, but it needs to be in `guild.json`'s plugin list and the guild needs to be started. There's no `nsg oculus` or `nsg serve` command. **This needs a followup** — at minimum, an `nsg serve` CLI command or adding Oculus to the default plugin set.

My recommendation: **the startup mechanism is the only thing worth a followup commission.** The rest is livable.

## Dashboard Comparison & Experiments

Great question. **X013** is the natural home — it's literally about what predicts good outcomes, and this is the cleanest natural experiment we have:

- **Same task domain** (web dashboard)
- **Same complexity** (20)
- **Different spec quality** (weak vs strong)
- **Dramatically different outcomes** (broken/partial vs clean/success)

This is a strong data point for X013 H1 (Spec Quality Predicts Output Quality). The confounds are minimal — same type of work, same complexity rating, the major variable is spec quality.

**X014** (Technical Spec Quality) is also relevant but from a different angle — the Oculus spec was highly prescriptive (full type definitions, pseudocode, helper function signatures), which blurs the line between "mountain spec" and "trail map." That's X014's core question: does that level of detail matter, or would a mountain-quality spec have worked just as well?

Want me to add an observation note to X013's artifacts documenting the dashboard-vs-dashboard comparison? And should we draft a brief for an `nsg serve` or similar startup command?

message.content: yes, add the x013 note. then just add `nsg oculus` as a startup command -- i want to see it running asap so skipping commissions here

content.text: Now I understand the pattern. The Oculus needs a tool that starts the apparatus. But wait — apparatuses start when the guild boots. The issue is that `createGuild` in the CLI just loads plugins, it doesn't call `start()` on apparatuses. Let me check how the guild lifecycle works:

So `createGuild` *does* start apparatuses. That means when the CLI boots and calls `createGuild(home)`, if Oculus is in the plugin list, it'll call `start()` which binds the HTTP server — and then the CLI proceeds to parse commands and exits. The server would start and immediately die when the process exits.

The `nsg oculus` command needs to be a **long-running command** that starts the guild and keeps the process alive. Let me look at how crawl-continual handles this:

# Dashboard vs Dashboard — Natural Experiment

Date: 2026-04-07

## Setup

Two commissions for the same domain (web dashboard), same complexity rating
(20), dispatched months apart with different spec quality.

| Field | w-mni87qen (Mk 1) | w-mnorf3k0 (Oculus) |
|---|---|---|
| Title | Web Dashboard | The Oculus — Web Dashboard Apparatus |
| Complexity | 20 | 20 |
| Spec quality (pre) | weak | strong |
| Outcome | partial | success |
| Failure mode | broken | — |
| Revision required | yes | no |
| Tests | 0 | 44 |
| Fatal bugs | JS syntax error in onclick handlers | None |
| Lines of code | ~1000 (monolith) | 537 + 225 CSS + 43 types + 28 index |
| Codebase consistency (blind) | 3.00 | 5.00 |
| Composite (blind) | — | 4.33 (σ 0.12) |
| Composite (aware) | — | 4.67 (σ 0.25) |
| Integration | — | 4.17 (σ 0.24) |
| Revise session | — | 13-second no-op ($0.06) |

## Observations

### Spec quality is the dominant variable

The first dashboard had a weak spec — deliberately vague, exploratory. The
anima produced an impressive amount of work (full SPA with 5 tabs, sortable
tables, modals, API layer) but with a fatal JS syntax error: escaped quotes
inside template literal onclick handlers broke all writ action buttons. No
tests. A 1000-line html.ts monolith. The codebase_consistency score was
perfect (3.00 on the old scale) — the anima knew how to be a good plugin,
it just produced buggy output.

The second dashboard had a strong spec — 495 lines with full type
definitions, helper function signatures, explicit validation checklist, and
comprehensive test cases. The anima produced clean, well-structured code
with 44 tests covering all major behaviors. The reviewer found nothing to
fix. The revise session was a 13-second no-op.

### Confounds

- **Time gap**: ~5 days apart. Framework matured significantly between
  commissions — more apparatus patterns to follow, better plugin
  conventions established.
- **Model**: both used the same model class, but the second had access to
  a more mature codebase for in-context learning.
- **Rig evolution**: the first commission used an earlier, simpler rig
  without the implement→review→revise cycle. The second used the full
  three-session rig (though the revise session was a no-op).
- **Spec detail level**: the second spec was not just "strong" in the X013
  mountain-quality sense — it was prescriptive, with pseudocode and type
  definitions. This is an X014 (Technical Spec Quality) data point, not
  purely X013.

### Relevance to X013 H1

This is a strong data point for H1 (Spec Quality Predicts Output Quality).
The dominant variable is spec quality; the outcome difference is
categorical (broken vs clean). The confounds (time, rig maturity) exist
but the magnitude of the outcome difference exceeds what those factors
alone would explain — the first commission's fatal bug was in basic
JavaScript syntax, not in system integration or framework understanding.

### Relevance to X014

The Oculus spec was highly prescriptive — closer to pseudocode than a
mountain-quality spec. The anima's job was essentially transcription. This
is the same pattern seen in the Copilot commission (w-mnolvtcc, cx 8):
detailed spec → near-zero revision. The open question from X014: would a
mountain-quality spec at the same complexity have produced comparable
results, or does complexity 20 require this level of detail?

# Coco Log — Standing Research Instrument
#
# One entry per unit of work handled directly by Coco (the interactive
# collaborator) rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission.
# Parallel to the commission log — together they capture the full
# picture of how work is routed in the system.
#
# An entry is created for each commit (or tight cluster of commits
# toward a single goal) that Coco produces during a session.
#
# Fields:
#   session        — Coco's Claude session ID (join key to archived transcripts)
#   date           — date the work was done (YYYY-MM-DD)
#   item           — short description of the work (one line)
#   commissionable — whether this work could have been an autonomous commission
#   justification  — why it can't be commissioned (required when commissionable is false)
#
# Justifications (required when commissionable: false):
#   interactive     — required real-time iteration with Sean
#   sanctum         — sanctum/experiment/meta work outside the commission boundary
#   decisional      — output was a decision, not an artifact

content.text: So valid variables are `$writ` and `$vars.<key>`. The guild.json uses `$role` and `$spider.buildCommand` etc. which are old syntax. Let me update:
So `$role` needs to become `$vars.role` and `$spider.buildCommand`/`$spider.testCommand` need to become `$vars.buildCommand`/`$vars.testCommand`. And the spider config needs a `variables` section:

# Patron Input Block Type

## Summary

Add a `patron-input` block type to the Spider so engines can pose structured questions to the patron, block until answered, and resume with the responses. Includes the data model, block checker, seven CLI tools, YAML export/import, and the addition of `rigId` to `EngineRunContext`.

## Current State

**EngineRunContext** (`packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.ts` lines 26–45):

```typescript
export interface EngineRunContext {
  /** Simple string identity for this engine instance (e.g. 'draft', 'implement'). */
  engineId: string;
  /** All upstream yields, keyed by engine id. */
  upstream: Record<string, unknown>;
  priorBlock?: {
    type: string;
    condition: unknown;
    blockedAt: string;
    message?: string;
    lastCheckedAt?: string;
  };
}
```

**Existing block types** (`packages/plugins/spider/src/block-types/`): `writ-status`, `scheduled-time`, `book-updated`. All follow the same pattern — standalone module exporting a `BlockType` object with `id`, `conditionSchema`, `pollIntervalMs`, and `check()`.

**Spider supportKit** (`packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts` lines 853–872) declares one book (`rigs`), five engines, three block types, and six tools. Block types are registered via the `blockTypes` key on `supportKit`; the Spider's `consumes: ['blockTypes']` mechanism scans kits for this key at startup.

**Context assembly** happens at two sites in `spider.ts`:
- `tryRun()` line ~595: `const context = { engineId: pending.id, upstream, ...(priorBlock ? { priorBlock } : {}) };`
- `tryCollect()` line ~436: `const context = { engineId: engine.id, upstream };`

Neither currently passes `rigId`.

## Requirements

- R1: `EngineRunContext` in `@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus` must include a `rigId: string` field, positioned before `engineId`.
- R2: The Spider must pass `rigId: rig.id` in every `EngineRunContext` it constructs — both in `tryRun()` and `tryCollect()`.
- R3: New types must be added to `packages/plugins/spider/src/types.ts`: `InputRequestStatus`, `QuestionType`, `ChoiceQuestionSpec`, `BooleanQuestionSpec`, `TextQuestionSpec`, `QuestionSpec`, `ChoiceAnswer`, `AnswerValue`, and `InputRequestDoc`.
- R4: The Spider must declare an `input-requests` book in `supportKit.books` with indexes `['status', 'rigId', 'engineId', 'createdAt', ['rigId', 'engineId', 'status']]`.
- R5: A `patron-input` block type must be registered in `supportKit.blockTypes`. It must poll every 10 seconds, accept condition `{ requestId: string }`, and return `cleared` when the request status is `completed`, `failed` (with reason) when `rejected`, `failed` when the document is not found, and `pending` otherwise.
- R6: The `input-request-answer` tool must accept choice answers via mutually exclusive `--select` and `--custom` params. `selected` must be validated against the question's options map. `custom` must only be accepted when `allowCustom` is true. Boolean and text answers use a `--value` param; boolean values must be `'true'` or `'false'`.
- R7: The `input-request-complete` tool must throw an error listing unanswered question keys when any questions lack answers. When all questions are answered, it must transition status to `completed` and return the updated document.
- 

... [20012 characters truncated] ...

alidation (choice):**
- `{ selected: 'validKey' }` with key in options → accepted
- `{ selected: 'missingKey' }` with key not in options → throws
- `{ custom: 'text' }` with `allowCustom: true` → accepted
- `{ custom: 'text' }` with `allowCustom: false` → throws
- `{}` (neither key) → throws
- `{ selected: 'a', custom: 'b' }` (both keys) → throws

**Answer validation (boolean):**
- `true` → `true`
- `false` → `false`
- `'true'` → `true` (string coercion)
- `'false'` → `false` (string coercion)
- `'yes'` → throws
- `42` → throws

**Answer validation (text):**
- `'hello'` → `'hello'`
- `''` (empty string) → accepted
- `42` → throws

**input-request-answer tool:**
- Answering a choice question with `--select validKey` → answer is `{ selected: 'validKey' }`
- Answering a choice question with `--custom text` where `allowCustom: true` → answer is `{ custom: 'text' }`
- Providing both `--select` and `--custom` → throws
- Providing neither `--select`, `--custom`, nor `--value` → throws
- Providing `--select` for a boolean question → throws
- Providing `--value true` for a boolean question → answer is `true`
- Providing `--value text` for a text question → answer is `'text'`
- Answering on a completed request → throws
- Answering a non-existent question key → throws
- Overwriting a previous answer while pending → succeeds with new value

**input-request-complete tool:**
- All questions answered → status becomes `completed`, returns document
- One question unanswered → throws listing the key
- Multiple questions unanswered → throws listing all keys
- Completing an already-completed request → throws
- Completing a rejected request → throws

**input-request-reject tool:**
- Reject with reason → status `rejected`, `rejectionReason` set
- Reject without reason → status `rejected`, no `rejectionReason`
- Reject after partial answers → succeeds
- Reject a completed request → throws

**input-request-list tool:**
- Default (no params) → returns only pending requests
- `--status completed` → returns only completed requests
- Empty result set → returns empty array

**input-request-export/import:**
- Export a request → YAML contains `id`, `questions`, `answers`, `message`
- Export → edit answers in file → import → answers updated
- Import with invalid choice key → throws
- Import targeting a completed request → throws
- Import with missing `id` in YAML → throws

**End-to-end (block lifecycle):**
- Engine creates request, returns blocked → checker returns pending → patron answers all questions → patron completes → checker returns cleared → engine resumes with `priorBlock` set → engine queries book by `rigId + engineId + status: 'completed'` and retrieves answers
- Engine creates request, returns blocked → patron rejects → checker returns failed → rig fails → writ transitions to failed

**rigId on EngineRunContext:**
- Existing block type tests still pass with `rigId` present
- Engine `run()` receives `context.rigId` matching the rig's id
- Engine `collect()` receives `context.rigId` matching the rig's id",

# The Oculus — Web Dashboard Apparatus

## Summary

A new apparatus (`@shardworks/oculus-apparatus`) that serves a web dashboard for the guild via Hono. Plugins contribute pages as static asset directories and custom API routes through kit contributions. Guild tools are automatically exposed as REST endpoints. A shared stylesheet and injected navigation chrome give contributed pages a cohesive appearance with zero boilerplate. Prerequisite: the `ToolCaller` type's `'cli'` value is renamed to `'patron'` across the codebase.

```typescript

```

The `'cli'` value is used in `callableBy` declarations across ~19 files (CLI framework commands, animator/summon, tests). The CLI filters tools via:

## Summary

Here's what we did, Sean:

### Commission Review
- **w-mnorf3k0 (Oculus): success** — all 22 requirements, 44 tests, typecheck clean
- Instrument scores: blind 4.33, aware 4.67, integration 4.17
- Main ding: `utility_reuse: 3.33` — duplicated coercion/param-extraction logic, but the spec *told* the anima to reimplement rather than import

### `nsg oculus` Startup Tool
Added a `supportKit.tools` entry to the Oculus apparatus — a one-line `oculus` tool that prints the URL and blocks until Ctrl+C. The server is already running from `apparatus.start()`, so the tool just holds the process open.

Also had to fix the vibers `guild.json` — it still had old `$role` / `$spider.buildCommand` variable syntax from before the template variable rename. Updated to `$vars.role` / `$vars.buildCommand` / `$vars.testCommand` with a `variables` block.

**It's running!** 37 tools exposed as REST endpoints. You can hit `http://localhost:7470/api/writ/list`, `http://localhost:7470/api/_tools`, the home page, etc. No contributed pages yet (no plugins have page contributions), but the framework is wired.

### X013 Observation
Added `2026-04-07-dashboard-comparison.md` to X013 artifacts — documents the natural experiment: same domain, same complexity, different spec quality, dramatically different outcomes. Notes confounds (time gap, rig maturity, spec detail level) but concludes the magnitude of the outcome difference supports H1.
